words: This second conversation with KATE SANDERSON WALDRON took place at her home
in Bovill, Idaho on August 25, 1976. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.

speaker: SS
words: What was it like in your family? How your parents raised you.

speaker: SS
words: This is something we like to ask people because it's so different

speaker: SS
words: it seems nowdays than it used to be

speaker: SS
words: KATE WALDRON: Well, my mother was a devout Chrisitan and my brother, John,

speaker: SS
words: was converted - did he ever tell you about his conversion?

speaker: SS
words: Actually I didn't ask him about that, but you talked to me about

speaker: SS
words: it when I was here.

speaker: KW
words: He was a Christian, and he's the oldest one in the family. My

speaker: KW
words: father was working as a timber cruiser, I guess it was for the Potlatch,

speaker: KW
words: and of course, he wasn't home too much of the time. He was

speaker: KW
words: home at times, but he was gone most of the time. So, it was Mama

speaker: KW
words: and my brother, John, and we had a Christian home. We were raised

speaker: KW
words: in a Christian home. We went to church and Sunday School every

speaker: KW
words: Sunday. And when Dad was home, my dad was just like Byers, he

speaker: KW
words: just loved to cook, and he was a good cook, and he'd always have

speaker: KW
words: Sunday dinner and we could always ask company home for Sunday

speaker: KW
words: dinner, because Mama knew that Dad had a good dinner going. MY

speaker: KW
words: dad was an awfully nice man. He was raised a Southern gentleman.

speaker: KW
words: His mother died in the Civil War, she had a baby and this baby was

speaker: KW
words: only three weeks old and they were crossing a river in the East

speaker: KW
words: there, I don't remember where it was, but in the Civil War and

speaker: KW
words: the bridge was burning at the time they crossed it, and it went

speaker: KW
words: down with them. And my grandmother was drowned- or nearly drownedshe

speaker: KW
words: took pneumonia, her baby was only three weeks old and they saved

speaker: KW
words: the baby bytmy grandmother died. That was my father's mother.

speaker: KW
words: And he was taken by an aunt and raised in the South. And he loved

speaker: KW
words: his dear old Negro Mammy, he just loved her, he said, "I couldn't

speaker: KW
words: have had a finer mother than that Negro woman." We were raised to

speaker: KW
words: respect the Negro people. There was no class hatred in our family,

speaker: KW
words: because my dad loved the Negro people because he was raised by such

speaker: KW
words: a fine Negro woman. That is, she was the nurse, and the aunt that

speaker: KW
words: raised him.

speaker: SS
words: Was he Swedish? I thought that he came from Swedish people?

speaker: KW
words: The name indicates that, but that's not so. His father was a full

speaker: KW
words: blooded Englishman and h$$. mother was a fullblooded French woman, she

speaker: KW
words: was a French girl. And all during school years there was quite an

speaker: KW
words: argument. Every time I was in high school we had a teacher and

speaker: KW
words: they'd think that we were Swedish people, but we were not. I can't

speaker: KW
words: go ahead and explain why, but my dad did.

speaker: SS
words: From what part of the South then did he grow up in?

speaker: KW
words: Oh, just down South, I don't know. I don't even remember.

speaker: SS
words: What state?

speaker: KW
words: I don't remember.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know the Wells family at all out here? The Negro family

speaker: SS
words: that lived in Deary?

speaker: KW
words: Well, I knew of them. I think Byers and John knew them because

speaker: KW
words: they were up here long before I was. John, especially. Byers

speaker: KW
words: wasn't, he came when I did, because he was just a boy, he's six

speaker: KW
words: years younger than I am. But, I remember the Wells, yes. I waited

speaker: KW
words: on the ladies when I was working in the store, I waited on those

speaker: KW
words: Negro ladies. They were nice women.. Nice to wait on,

speaker: SS
words: Speaking about your parents: what I was wondering about was- one

speaker: SS
words: thing- were they very strict with you, or would you say lenient?

speaker: SS
words: The way they raised you?

speaker: KW
words: The way we were raised?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: KW
words: Yes, indeed. My mother was a strict woman. We were taught obedience.

speaker: KW
words: And I tell you, if we got sassy, now, we were slapped down right

speaker: KW
words: now! Mother wouldn't put up with that at all. But we were taught

speaker: KW
words: right and wrong, and we were taught manners; good manners. Mother

speaker: KW
words: was particular about that. And we had a very happy home. We were,

speaker: KW
words: I think, an exceptionally happy home. I never quarreled with my

speaker: KW
words: brother, John, in my life and he's 92 years old and. And

speaker: KW
words: he has never said an unkind word to me in his life. Now, that's

speaker: KW
words: something! And I've lived right around him, too, t'wasn't as

speaker: KW
words: though we were separated, because we lived- and he was a marvelous

speaker: KW
words: brother. He was the oldest one in the family, and he was a wonder

speaker: KW
words: ful brother. In those days, you know, we burned woodstoves and

speaker: KW
words: we had a cellar and you had to go outdoors and go clear around

speaker: KW
words: the house down into the cellar to get the vegetables, you know,

speaker: KW
words: and if we girls would ask him, there was only two girls, and the

speaker: KW
words: rest were boys, why, he never ever[once refused to go. If we

speaker: KW
words: needed a pail of water, he got it; if we needed some wood for the

speaker: KW
words: stove, he got it. If my sister was making a cake and she needed

speaker: KW
words: sugar she'd ask him and he'd go clear downtown and get

speaker: KW
words: whatever she needed. He was a good man, a good boy. Of course,

speaker: KW
words: Byers was the baby of the family.

speaker: SS
words: That sounds unusual.

speaker: KW
words: It was unusual. We had a nice family. We were a very close family.

speaker: KW
words: After we were all married and Dad was up here working, we'd all

speaker: KW
words: come home, we lived in here, that'jj^hy we've all back in here,

speaker: KW
words: to be near home. And every Sunday my sister and I would make the

speaker: KW
words: cakes and the salads or the pies, whatever we were going to have,

speaker: KW
words: Mama would take care of the meat and the vegetables and we would

speaker: KW
words: all be at home.

speaker: SS
words: The whole family?

speaker: KW
words: Yes. The whole family and the grandchildren. So that was quite

speaker: KW
words: rare, too. Byers wasn't married at that time and John wasn't yet

speaker: KW
words: married. But after they were married it was the same thing, it

speaker: KW
words: was their families then.

speaker: SS
words: Tell me what you think about why- what it was that made your family

speaker: SS
words: a close one.

speaker: KW
words: Well, I think it was our raising, our teaching. And my brother,

speaker: KW
words: John, was a Christian, a devout Christian and my mother was a

speaker: KW
words: Christian, and we associated with the best of people; the church

speaker: KW
words: people. And the doctors and his wife were great friends of my mother's

speaker: KW
words: and father's.. Nice people. They didn't go to the same

speaker: KW
words: church as we did, but my father and Doc , were the greatest

speaker: KW
words: of friends. And Doc was our doctor. And we were just raised

speaker: KW
words: and educated in Palouse. But we were a nice family. We were a

speaker: KW
words: close family. And I know that's why my husband wanted to come

speaker: KW
words: back here his health broke and he was sick; he wanted to come

speaker: KW
words: back to Bovill. I know now it was because he wanted to leave me

speaker: KW
words: close to my family; I know that's the reason because we were living

speaker: KW
words: in Moscow and had been there for about twelve years; ten or twelve

speaker: KW
words: years. He wanted to come back home so I- we owned this home. We

speaker: KW
words: were only here two years when he was taken sick and died. We

speaker: KW
words: weren't old enough for our social security, so I went to work. We

speaker: KW
words: moved to Moscow and I went to work in Moscow; I clerked for

speaker: KW
words: years. And then when we got oldenough to draw our social security

speaker: KW
words: we came back here.

speaker: SS
words: When did you first meet him?

speaker: KW
words: I met him the 29th of March on an Easter Sunday and we went walking.

speaker: KW
words: We didn't have cars in those days. And there*d be a group

speaker: KW
words: of us girls, we'd get together and we'd go out walking. And gen

speaker: KW
words: erally that early in the spring we'd just walk up the railroad

speaker: KW
words: track for a nice walk. And there was three of us and we were

speaker: KW
words: walking up the track and Ray was just from Minnesota he'd

speaker: KW
words: come out here to be with his brother, their mother had died.

speaker: KW
words: And he came out here to be with his older brother, and he was working

speaker: KW
words: in the sawmill there in Palouse. And another nice fellow

speaker: KW
words: and they come walking up the track behind us. The two girls that

speaker: KW
words: were with me, first one would turn around and look back and the

speaker: KW
words: boys were laughing and pretty soon the other one turned around

speaker: KW
words: and looked back and they said, "Well, now, just wait, the third

speaker: KW
words: girl'11 turn around and look back." And I was the third girl.

speaker: KW
words: And Ray, my husband,Asays, "Well, I'll bet you a dollar that that

speaker: KW
words: girl will never turn around." And they bet with him and I didn't

speaker: KW
words: turn around! (Chuckles) Well, they finally caught up with us and TaV

speaker: KW
words: introduced me to his brother, Ray, and Ray was from the East.

speaker: KW
words: I had three or four girlfriends- Ray had been in town a couple of

speaker: KW
words: weeks- and the girls^a lot of 'en^had met him and Iwas at her

speaker: KW
words: home and there was a group of us there, and they said, "We've got

speaker: KW
words: one boy that you're not going to meet, Miss Kitty," they called

speaker: KW
words: me Kitty, my name was Katherine, and they called me Kate and Kitty,

speaker: KW
words: the girls called me Kitty, and I said, "Well, who's that?" And

speaker: KW
words: they said','lt's Jay's brother, and we're going to make sure .

speaker: KW
words: you don't meet him." And Isaid, "I don't^o meet him." Isaid,

speaker: KW
words: "I'm not interested in him anyway. I don't care." I had a steady

speaker: KW
words: fellow, but my steady fellow had taken off for Texas. He was from

speaker: KW
words: Texas, and he was gone at the time I met Ray. I was the girl that

speaker: KW
words: Jay wanted his brother to go with, and that's why he wanted me to

speaker: KW
words: meet his brother, that's why they were following us up the track.

speaker: KW
words: I learned this all later. I didn't know it, of course, at the time.

speaker: KW
words: But I was the girl that Jay wanted his brother to meet. And I

speaker: KW
words: knew Jay, and so I met Ray. Well, it didn't take me long when I

speaker: KW
words: got with those girls, I said, "You girls think you're so smart,

speaker: KW
words: but I'tell you I met that kid." And they said, "Where did you meet

speaker: KW
words: him? We didn't introduce you." And I said, "You didn't have to,

speaker: KW
words: I met him last Sunday, his brother, Jay, introduced me to him."

speaker: KW
words: And Ray and I went together and my boyfriend came back from Texas,

speaker: KW
words: but I liked Ray much the best and I went with Ray.

speaker: SS
words: How old were you when you started dating?

speaker: KW
words: Sixteen. I was sixteen.

speaker: SS
words: What did you do when you went out together in those days? What

speaker: SS
words: was the dating like?

speaker: KW
words: There wasn't really very many places to go, we'd just meet together

speaker: KW
words: in different homes, a group of us. That's the way we spent our

speaker: KW
words: time. And then we'd go walking together or out in a horse and bug

speaker: KW
words: gy, riding. My brother, John- that was another thing about my bro

speaker: KW
words: ther, John; he'd be going with some girl and he always liked to have

speaker: KW
words: Ray and I with him or the boy that I was going with, it didn't make

speaker: KW
words: any difference; it made four of us, see. John was so much older

speaker: KW
words: but he went with younger girls, it'd be girls along about my age.

speaker: KW
words: And that way, the four of us were always together.

speaker: SS
words: Was Ray much older than you?

speaker: KW
words: No, he was only six months; his birthday is in March and mine was

speaker: KW
words: in August.

speaker: SS
words: So he came out as a very young fellow?

speaker: KW
words: Yes, he was just a boy. He quit high school and he came out West

speaker: KW
words: to be with his brother; the mother was gone, you see, and he got

speaker: KW
words: Jay to send for him; give him money. Jay was quite a bit older

speaker: KW
words: than Ray. He was one of the oldest children of the family and

speaker: KW
words: Ray was next to the youngest. He went to work in the sawmill there.

speaker: KW
words: He tied bundles. Boarded at a fine home there; big man at the

speaker: KW
words: mill, Maxwell was his name, and he and his brother boarded with

speaker: KW
words: them. And then Ray and Jay were great friends of the big Potlatch

speaker: KW
words: man by the name of Kendall. And they had a boy that was a great

speaker: KW
words: friend of Jay's, they grew up together; those kids all grew up to

speaker: KW
words: gether, the Kendall kids. And Mr. and Mrs. Kendall and Ray's father

speaker: KW
words: and mother were great friends. And of course, when the boys came

speaker: KW
words: out West here the Kendalls just put 'em right into jobs you

speaker: KW
words: see. It was a big pull for them.

speaker: SS
words: What did the Kendalls- what was their position with Potlatch? Do

speaker: SS
words: you remember?

speaker: KW
words: No, but he was one of the head men. And Frank, his son, became aone

speaker: KW
words: of the big men. They were situated in Spokane. They were in

speaker: KW
words: Palouse for a long time, but they finally were in Spokane. But

speaker: KW
words: they were big men of the Potlatch for years until they died. I

speaker: KW
words: have a deck of cards here- the largest white pine tree in the world

speaker: KW
words: was cut here in Bovill and Frank Kendall sent that to my husband

speaker: KW
words: when that tree was cut down. I showed that to somebody, I haven't

speaker: KW
words: any idea who, and I had the card that was in it that told of the

speaker: KW
words: feet of lumber and when it was cut and everything.

speaker: SS
words: That's beautiful. I really like that.

speaker: KW
words: And I have a piece of wood, that the day they cut that tree that

speaker: KW
words: my husband was right there and this is a piece of the wood from

speaker: KW
words: the tree. I don't know whether you can read the writing on it or

speaker: KW
words: not, it's getting to old, it's sixty-five or sixty-six years old,

speaker: KW
words: you see. I'd give my eyeteeth to know who stole my card that was in

speaker: KW
words: here. I've got a few other cards in here that I got from the Wanagon-

speaker: KW
words: you know what a wanagon is?

speaker: SS
words: Isn't that -

speaker: KW
words: That river boat. I had an 8x10 picture of it, but my nephew,

speaker: KW
words: Chet Morris, knew the men that were in the logging on that river

speaker: KW
words: the last trip that they made, and he didn't get one, and he knew

speaker: KW
words: those men and had worked with them, and I gave him that picture.

speaker: SS
words: What was it about him that made you like him so much better than

speaker: SS
words: this fellow from Texas?

speaker: KW
words: (Chuckles) Well, I liked the fellow from Texas, he was a dandy,

speaker: KW
words: nice kid. I often think about ., we called him . His mother

speaker: KW
words: died on the trip from Texas. His father come up and got work up

speaker: KW
words: in this country and he brought his mother and his two little sis

speaker: KW
words: ters from Texas, and his mother died on the trip. She took sick

speaker: KW
words: and died, just instantly, a heart attack or something like that.

speaker: KW
words: And he was only seventeen then, and he brought his mother and his

speaker: KW
words: little sisters and he went to work there in Palouse. And was just

speaker: KW
words: a A-number I kid; he was a nice boy.

speaker: SS
words: What was it about Ray that was special?

speaker: KW
words: Ray was kinda- he'd been raised differently. I could tell that

speaker: KW
words: even though I was just a girl. His father was a traveling man.

speaker: KW
words: He traveled for the Mc Cormick Company. I don't know whether that

speaker: KW
words: company still is today or not, but it's a big company. And he was

speaker: KW
words: a very attractive man. He used to take first one boy and then the

speaker: KW
words: other^a lot with him traveling. And Ray had beautiful manners.

speaker: KW
words: He was polished. That travel, you know gives him a polish that

speaker: KW
words: us ordinary kids don't get because we don't travel around. And

speaker: KW
words: I think that's what fascinated me. And then he had a nice per

speaker: KW
words: sonality, and he was sweet and kind. He was a nice sweetheart.

speaker: KW
words: Very polite and thoughtful. He was the kind of a fellow that a

speaker: KW
words: girl would like. Women always did like him! (Chuckles) Even af

speaker: KW
words: ter Iwas married, women liked

speaker: SS
words: How old were you when you got married?

speaker: KW
words: Eighteen. Ray was only nineteen.

speaker: SS
words: Did your parents—

speaker: KW
words: We were married and lived with his father down in Southern Idaho.

speaker: KW
words: My children were born in Southern Idaho. And his father then came

speaker: KW
words: out West and became a real estate man and we lived there. And

speaker: KW
words: Dad had 160 acres of land down there, in Southern Idaho, in Parma.

speaker: KW
words: We were down there until- We came to Bovill when my little girl

speaker: KW
words: was six months old. But work just wasn't plentiful down there

speaker: KW
words: in Southern Idaho, and I knew that my father held a position here

speaker: KW
words: that if Ray and I come up here my dad would see that Ray would get

speaker: KW
words: a good job, you know; so we came up here.

speaker: SS
words: At that time and at that age did you feel that you were well pre

speaker: SS
words: pared to raise a family?

speaker: KW
words: Oh, I guess so. I dearly loved my youngsters and when my little

speaker: KW
words: girl was three years old I started a Sunday School of my own. I

speaker: KW
words: financed it. And we lived in what's called the Old Slab Town.

speaker: KW
words: There were a number of families there; they even had a school out

speaker: KW
words: there, there were so many children. And I had about fourteen and

speaker: KW
words: I had little Catholic children; I had lots of little Catholic

speaker: KW
words: children.

speaker: SS
words: I'm thinking of- well, you know nowdays, a lot of young people

speaker: SS
words: feel they need to have a whole lot of resources, to start life.

speaker: KW
words: I didn't feel that way. What I had was nice, but we left all of

speaker: KW
words: our furniture down in Southern Idaho and I lived in a little two

speaker: KW
words: room shack up there at that Camp 8, just like all the other women

speaker: KW
words: did. We just had little houses to live in. I had one great, big

speaker: KW
words: room.

speaker: SS
words: i didn't realize that wives were living at Camp 8 along with the

speaker: SS
words: men. But they were?

speaker: KW
words: Yes. You see was the main shop that my dad had there,

speaker: KW
words: and at one time he had 700 men under him there.

speaker: SS
words: At Camp g?

speaker: KW
words: Uh-huh. Camp 8. John's father-in-law was foreman of that camp,

speaker: KW
words: Camp 8, for a long time, too.

speaker: SS
words: Were there many married men at camp? Any women living there?

speaker: KW
words: yes, we had- there was enough of children so that we had a school

speaker: KW
words: at Camp 8.

speaker: SS
words: was Slab Town and Camp 8 pretty much the same, or was Slab Town in

speaker: SS
words: a different place? Was Camp 8 right at Slab Town?

speaker: KW
words: Well, yes. It was about like walking downtown here. We were that

speaker: KW
words: close. I was trying in my mind to count the houses. But there was

speaker: KW
words: quite a number of houses, because Dad had a house out there, too.

speaker: KW
words: Dad had quite a big house. And we lived out there for two or three

speaker: KW
words: years. Then when the forest fire came, that was in 1914, after

speaker: KW
words: the forest five came I moved downtown here. I was the first woman

speaker: KW
words: in this town that had hot and cold water. And oh, boy! was that

speaker: KW
words: something. I was so happy. The people moved away and wanted to

speaker: KW
words: rent their house, that owned the house, and I rented it and I

speaker: KW
words: don't remember what rent I paid, but it was big rent for then in

speaker: KW
words: those days, and my brother, Frank, he's dead now, he just had

speaker: KW
words: a fit! (Chuckles) He just thought I was crazy to pay that much rent

speaker: KW
words: But it wasn't very much; $15, $20, I don't know what it was, I

speaker: KW
words: don't remember. But I Frank thought- Frank 3 younger

speaker: KW
words: than i am- and he w^as married, and I dont know whether he wanted

speaker: KW
words: the house and was mad because I got it or not.

speaker: SS
words: Did that house have hot and cold running water in it at the time

speaker: SS
words: you got it?

speaker: KW
words: Yes at the time, that's why I rented it, was because it had hot

speaker: KW
words: and cold water.

speaker: SS
words: When you were out at Camp 8; was there much of a social life?

speaker: KW
words: Ob, yes, yes. We came downtown to everything.

speaker: SS
words: To Bovill?

speaker: KW
words: Uh-huh. We could get on a train and come down here and do our

speaker: KW
words: shopping, and it would give us two, three hours downtown here and

speaker: KW
words: then*at four o'clock it went back. And that gave us time to get

speaker: KW
words: nome to get dinner; supper. And we belonged to everything

speaker: KW
words: down here; all the clubs that they had, we belonged to all the

speaker: KW
words: dances and things like that; shows and everything we came down.

speaker: KW
words: Didn t have any cars, but we got down here, even if we had to walk!

speaker: KW
words: It wasn't too far. I walked it many, many times. But we had social

speaker: KW
words: life. It's always been, it is now. I was out to a big

speaker: KW
words: party yesterday. And my ladyfriend who lives there in that trailer,

speaker: KW
words: she had a beautiful party. She had a lot of people from Deary.

speaker: KW
words: And there's lots going on here all the time. The kids go to Moscow

speaker: KW
words: and bowl. They have two different bowling leagues and they play ten

speaker: KW
words: nis downtown, the ladies, even the postmistress plays tennis down

speaker: KW
words: there. And they have their church activities. There's two classes

speaker: KW
words: of people here; there's the tavern class}then there's the church

speaker: KW
words: class of people. There's two distinct classes. But they're not

speaker: KW
words: uppish or hateful towards each other. But there's a lot of 'em that

speaker: KW
words: never darken a church door. And there's a lot of us that do, but

speaker: KW
words: we're not enemies by any means.

speaker: SS
words: Was it the same way back in the teens?

speaker: KW
words: Yes. Yes, it was the same way. It's always been the same way.

speaker: KW
words: But they had bridge clubs and pinochle clubs and forget-me-not

speaker: KW
words: clubs. And at Christmastime I'm usually invited to all kind of

speaker: KW
words: parties, all the time, except the card parties, I don't play cards.

speaker: KW
words: Of course, they fill their tables with their friends that play

speaker: KW
words: cards, but the other clubs, I'm always invited.

speaker: SS
words: Are there people in Bovill that belong to both groups, or does it

speaker: SS
words: have to be one or the other? Either tavern or church.

speaker: KW
words: Well, I suppose. I go to church in Moscow, every Sunday we go

speaker: KW

words: it up. We used to give smorgasbords and people came from all over

words: so good and they put on a smorgasbord. And we had a beautiful recreation room here

speaker: KW
words: downtown that the Potlatch made.

speaker: KW

speaker: KW
words: people. You know they are chilly and they like laprobe* And

words: that's the project that we're on now. And they give food sales.

speaker: KW
words: - and she gives some wonderful lessons on religion on the Bible.

words: She's very capable, very able. She's really the finest they've

words: ever had here and I've been here a long time.

words: For these people, you know. They'd want us to because were

speaker: KW
words: (You know, I just got my arm out of the cast a couple of weeks ago.

words: care of me. That's Mrs. Chet Morris. And she was my baby. When

words: stay down to her house, because I wanted to be home. I wan

speaker: KW
words: ted my own bed and I knew it would make it much easier for her, if

words: all the time.

speaker: KW

words: there in Moscow.
